title: Branch handle type and fork operation
purpose: Deliver the BranchHandle type and the fork operation that creates it. A fork records the current root hash as the fork-point and allocates empty per-shard WAL buffers, sharing the parent's content-addressed tree nodes. This is the foundation for all branching operations — merge, time-travel, and pruning all build on the handle this brief creates.
task: Define the BranchHandle type holding fork-point root hash, current root hash, and per-shard WAL buffer references. Implement fork() that records the current root hash and allocates empty WAL buffers without copying any tree nodes. Fork operates per-shard: each shard gets its own fork-point and branch buffer. Branch reads check the branch WAL buffer first, then the shared tree. This brief builds the fork primitive only — merge is BRANCH-002, snapshots are BRANCH-003.

requirements.title: Branch module structure
requirements.spec: Create the branch module with mod.rs containing only pub mod declarations and pub use re-exports. Create handle.rs and fork.rs as sub-modules.

crates/haematite/src/branch/handle.rs exists
crates/haematite/src/branch/fork.rs exists
cargo check -p haematite succeeds

context: The files listed as “create” in the brief already exist in this worktree; treat them as skeletons to fill, not absent paths.

branch/mod.rs currently satisfies the structural constraint of containing only pub mod and pub use statements, but it includes modules for later briefs and currently re-exports only checkout/snapshot APIs.
lib.rs follows the convention of declaring crate modules at the top and then re-exporting public API types/functions in grouped pub use blocks.
scout.approach: Keep branch/mod.rs declarative: add pub use lines for BranchHandle, BranchRegistry, and fork-related functions/types without adding implementation code or tests there. In lib.rs, extend the existing pub use branch::{...} block with the new branch API so downstream users can import from haematite directly. Do not remove the existing later-brief module declarations unless they fail cargo check; this repo already contains those files, and the acceptance wording is about statement kinds, not limiting the module list to only handle/fork.
scout.notes: R1 says “Create” but the files already exist. The main gotcha is to avoid adding helper constants/types/tests to mod.rs; place all logic in handle.rs/fork.rs/registry.rs so the “only pub mod and pub use” acceptance remains true.

requirements.title: BranchHandle type
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define a BranchHandle type that holds: the fork-point root hash (the common ancestor), the current root hash (initially equal to fork-point), and per-shard WAL buffer references for the branch's own writes. THE SYSTEM SHALL implement Debug and Clone for BranchHandle. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT store copies of tree nodes — the handle references the shared content-addressed store.
acceptance: BranchHandle has a fork_point() method returning the fork-point root hash
BranchHandle has a current_root() method returning the current root hash
BranchHandle has a shard_buffer(shard_id) method returning a reference to the branch's WAL buffer for that shard
On creation, current_root() equals fork_point()
BranchHandle implements Debug and Clone

files: crates/haematite/src/branch/handle.rs:1-1 — empty placeholder where BranchHandle should live.
crates/haematite/src/tree/node.rs:9-24 — Hash is Copy/Clone/Debug/Eq/Hash and is the root-hash type to store, not node data.
crates/haematite/src/wal/buffer.rs:116-180 — WalBuffer is Debug+Default but not Clone; it provides new(), put/delete/get, and LookupResult.
crates/haematite/src/wal/buffer.rs:182-197 — WalBuffer exposes len/is_empty/iter for tests and buffer inspection.
crates/haematite/src/store/memory.rs:7-13 — NodeStore trait shows tree nodes live in stores; BranchHandle should not own/copy them.
context: Hash is a small Copy value, so fork_point/current_root can be stored by value without violating the no-node-copy rule.

WalBuffer does not implement Clone, so a Clone BranchHandle cannot hold plain WalBuffer values unless WalBuffer gains Clone; prefer shared references such as Arc<Mutex<WalBuffer>> or Arc<RwLock<WalBuffer>> per shard.
Existing APIs use #[must_use] on simple constructors/accessors and return Hash by value for root getters.
scout.approach: Define BranchHandle around per-shard state, for example a Vec or HashMap keyed by shard id whose entries include fork_point: Hash, current_root: Hash, and Arc<Mutex<WalBuffer>>. To satisfy the brief’s singular fork_point()/current_root() methods while also supporting R5 per-shard roots, make the single-root methods return the primary/single-shard root and add per-shard accessors such as shard_fork_point(shard_id) and shard_current_root(shard_id) for R5 tests. Make shard_buffer(shard_id) return a clone of the Arc or an Option<&Arc<Mutex<WalBuffer>>>; returning a direct &WalBuffer is hard if branch writes must mutate it and BranchHandle must be Clone.
scout.notes: The phrase “returning a reference to the branch’s WAL buffer” conflicts slightly with Clone + mutable branch writes; use Arc<Mutex<WalBuffer>> and document that cloned handles reference the same branch buffers, not copied WAL data. Avoid deriving Clone if you add a Drop implementation for registry deregistration without thinking through clone semantics, because dropping one clone could deregister a still-live branch.

files_changed.note: Added BranchHandle, BranchError, ShardId, BranchWalBuffer, root accessors, shard buffer accessors, and Clone/Debug behavior.
Added BranchHandle tests outside handle.rs to keep branch files below the 500-line verification cap.

dev.how: BranchHandle stores only Hash values for fork/current roots plus per-shard Arc<Mutex<WalBuffer>> references, so cloned handles share branch-local buffers without copying tree nodes or WAL contents. The primary single-shard accessors fork_point(), current_root(), and shard_buffer(shard_id) are implemented, and per-shard root accessors support the multi-shard requirements.
dev.deviation: I followed the scout’s Arc<Mutex<WalBuffer>> approach. The only structural addition was handle_tests.rs for tests, mirroring the existing snapshot_tests.rs pattern to avoid bloating production handle.rs.

checklist.note: BranchHandle holds fork-point/current Hash values and per-shard shared WAL buffer references; no tree nodes are stored.

stories.note: Fork handles are constant-size root references plus empty per-shard buffers, supporting cheap experimental branches.

requirements.title: Fork operation
requirements.spec: WHEN fork() is called on a database or shard, THE SYSTEM SHALL record the current committed root hash as the fork-point and allocate empty per-shard WAL buffers for the branch. THE SYSTEM SHALL complete in O(1) time regardless of database size — no tree node copying. THE SYSTEM SHALL return a BranchHandle.
acceptance: fork() returns a BranchHandle with fork_point equal to the current committed root hash
fork() allocates one empty WAL buffer per shard
fork() does not read or copy any tree nodes
fork() on a database with 1 million entries completes in constant time (no O(n) work)

files: crates/haematite/src/branch/fork.rs:1-1 — placeholder for O(1) fork construction.
crates/haematite/src/branch/handle.rs:1-1 — fork() should construct and return BranchHandle from root hash(es) and empty buffers.
crates/haematite/src/wal/buffer.rs:121-126 — WalBuffer::new() is the empty buffer allocation primitive.
crates/haematite/src/tree/mutate.rs:41-57 — batch_mutate reads/rewrites tree nodes; fork must not call this or insert/delete.
crates/haematite/src/db.rs:1-12 — Database is currently only a stub, so database-level fork wiring may need a free function or small trait until real Database state exists.
context: There is no real Database root/shard state yet; db.rs is a zero-field struct with only comments about future commit-log wiring.
O(1) with respect to database entries means fork should only copy Hash values and allocate one empty buffer per shard; it may be O(number_of_shards).
Any call into Cursor, NodeStore::get, insert/delete, batch_mutate, or store.put during fork would undermine the acceptance criterion that no tree nodes are read or copied.
scout.approach: Implement fork as a pure constructor over already-known committed root hash(es), e.g. fork(root: Hash) -> BranchHandle for single-shard and fork_shards(roots: impl IntoIterator<Item=(ShardId, Hash)>) -> BranchHandle for per-shard use. Each shard entry should set current_root equal to fork_point and allocate WalBuffer::new() behind the chosen shared buffer reference type. If you add Database or shard methods, keep them thin wrappers that pass their current committed root hash into the free function; do not try to build missing database storage in this brief.
scout.notes: The “1 million entries constant time” test should be structural: use a large existing tree root or a counting/panic NodeStore and prove fork never touches it, rather than actually inserting a million entries in a unit test. Forking N shards necessarily allocates N empty buffers, so describe/test complexity as independent of entries and proportional only to shard count.

files_changed.note: Added fork(), fork_registered(), fork_shards(), and fork_shards_registered() constructors and tests; refactored tests to satisfy clippy::or_fun_call and clippy::significant_drop_tightening.
Added BranchHandle constructors consumed by fork helpers.

dev.how: fork(root) records the supplied committed root hash as both fork point and current root and allocates an empty WAL buffer through BranchHandle::new; multi-shard variants do the same once per supplied shard. The fork API accepts root hashes only and no NodeStore, so it cannot read, rewrite, or copy tree nodes and is O(1) with respect to database entry count.
dev.deviation: Because Database is still a zero-field stub and no canonical database registry exists, I exposed pure root-hash fork helpers plus registry-aware variants instead of inventing database storage/wiring.

checklist.note: fork records the current root hash as the fork point and allocates empty WAL buffers without node-store access.
Fork work is independent of entry count; it copies Hash values and allocates empty buffers only.

stories.note: Developers can create branches from root hashes without copying existing data.
A production root can be forked into an isolated handle with separate WAL buffers.

acceptance.criterion: fork() returns a BranchHandle with fork_point equal to the current committed root hash
acceptance.evidence: crates/haematite/src/branch/fork.rs:13-15 returns `BranchHandle::new(root)`; test `branch::fork::tests::fork_records_current_root_as_fork_point_and_current_root` passed.

acceptance.criterion: fork() allocates one empty WAL buffer per shard
acceptance.evidence: Single-shard fork delegates to `BranchShard::new(root)`, which creates `Arc::new(Mutex::new(WalBuffer::new()))` at crates/haematite/src/branch/handle.rs:293-299; multi-shard creation inserts a new `BranchShard` per shard at handle.rs:118-132; tests `fork_allocates_one_empty_buffer_for_single_shard` and `fork_shards_allocates_one_empty_buffer_per_shard` passed.

acceptance.criterion: fork() does not read or copy any tree nodes
acceptance.evidence: crates/haematite/src/branch/fork.rs:13-15 accepts only a `Hash` and calls `BranchHandle::new`; no `NodeStore` is accepted by fork helpers. Search of fork/handle/registry for boundary terms found no node-copying/commit logic; test `fork_single_shard_has_no_tree_store_to_read_or_copy` passed.

acceptance.criterion: fork() on a database with 1 million entries completes in constant time (no O(n) work)
acceptance.evidence: The implementation in crates/haematite/src/branch/fork.rs:13-15 copies a root hash and allocates branch state only; no database size or store traversal is in the API. Multi-shard work is proportional to shard count, not entry count, via handle.rs:109-135.

requirements.title: Independent branch read-write
requirements.spec: WHILE a branch exists, THE SYSTEM SHALL allow reads and writes to the branch without affecting the parent. Writes append to the branch's per-shard WAL buffers. Reads check the branch WAL buffer first and fall through to the shared tree if the key is not in the buffer. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT propagate branch mutations to the parent's WAL buffer or tree.
acceptance: A put on the branch does not appear in a get on the parent
A put on the parent after fork does not appear in a get on the branch
A get on the branch returns the branch buffer value for keys written after fork
A get on the branch returns the parent tree value for keys not written after fork

files: crates/haematite/src/branch/handle.rs:1-1 — branch put/get methods should live on BranchHandle.
crates/haematite/src/wal/buffer.rs:144-180 — WalBuffer put/delete/get semantics for shadowing tree values.
crates/haematite/src/wal/buffer.rs:32-41 — LookupResult distinguishes buffered value, buffered delete, and fallback-to-tree.
crates/haematite/src/tree/cursor.rs:31-67 — Cursor::new/get reads committed tree state at a root hash from a NodeStore.
crates/haematite/src/branch/checkout.rs:53-109 — adjacent example of a view holding a Cursor and reading directly from content-addressed storage.
context: WalBuffer::get already encodes the required read ordering: BufferedValue wins, BufferedDelete returns absence, NotBuffered means consult the tree.
Cursor provides the shared-tree fallback but requires a NodeStore reference and root hash; BranchHandle can either store a shared store reference/Arc or accept &S on get.
Existing checkout code documents the no-WAL-replay/direct-node-store model and is a good style reference for error handling and tests.
scout.approach: Add BranchHandle put/delete methods that select the correct shard buffer and mutate only that buffer. Add get that first locks/reads the selected branch WalBuffer and handles LookupResult; only for NotBuffered should it construct a Cursor over the shared store at that shard’s fork/current root and call Cursor::get. Given lifetime complexity, prefer methods like get<S: NodeStore + ?Sized>(&self, shard_id, store: &S, key: &[u8]) rather than storing borrowed NodeStore references inside a Clone handle.
scout.notes: Acceptance says parent writes after fork do not appear in the branch; this only holds if branch fallback uses the fork-point root, not a mutable parent current root. Also model deletes: a branch-side delete must shadow a parent tree value as None, not fall through.

files_changed.note: Added BranchHandle put, delete, and get methods with WAL-first lookup and shared-tree fallback.
Added isolation tests proving branch writes do not affect parent reads and post-fork parent writes do not affect branch reads.

dev.how: Branch writes mutate only the selected branch shard’s WalBuffer, while BranchHandle::get checks that buffer first and consults Cursor over the caller-provided shared NodeStore only on LookupResult::NotBuffered. Since fallback reads use the branch’s recorded root hash, parent mutations after fork are invisible to the branch, and branch mutations never touch the parent tree or WAL.

checklist.note: Tests cover independent branch/parent writes and branch-local buffer visibility.
Branch reads are explicitly WAL-first, then shared content-addressed tree fallback.

stories.note: A workflow history can be forked and written through a branch-local buffer for what-if reads.
Dropping/discarding a branch leaves parent tree state untouched because writes never propagate.
Branch fallback reads share the parent content-addressed tree by root hash rather than copying nodes.

acceptance.criterion: A put on the branch does not appear in a get on the parent
acceptance.evidence: crates/haematite/src/branch/handle.rs:195-203 writes only to the branch shard buffer. Test `branch::handle::tests::branch_put_does_not_affect_parent_tree` at crates/haematite/src/branch/handle_tests.rs:86-100 passed.

acceptance.criterion: A put on the parent after fork does not appear in a get on the branch
acceptance.evidence: crates/haematite/src/branch/handle.rs:242-247 falls back to `Cursor::new(store, root)` using the branch's recorded shard root, not the parent's later root. Test `branch_does_not_observe_parent_writes_after_fork` at handle_tests.rs:102-116 passed.

acceptance.criterion: A get on the branch returns the branch buffer value for keys written after fork
acceptance.evidence: crates/haematite/src/branch/handle.rs:234-241 checks the WAL buffer first and returns `BufferedValue`; test `branch_get_checks_buffer_before_shared_tree` at handle_tests.rs:118-135 passed.

acceptance.criterion: A get on the branch returns the parent tree value for keys not written after fork
acceptance.evidence: crates/haematite/src/branch/handle.rs:242-247 handles `LookupResult::NotBuffered` by reading the shared tree at the branch root; test `branch_get_checks_buffer_before_shared_tree` at handle_tests.rs:130-133 passed.

requirements.title: Per-shard fork independence
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL fork each shard independently: each shard gets its own fork-point root hash and its own branch WAL buffer. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT create cross-shard coordination during fork. THE SYSTEM SHALL coordinate per-shard forks through the BranchHandle so callers interact with the branch as a single logical database.
acceptance: Each shard in the branch has an independent fork-point root hash
Each shard in the branch has an independent WAL buffer
A write to shard 3 on the branch does not affect shard 5 on the branch
BranchHandle routes gets/puts to the correct shard's branch buffer

files: crates/haematite/src/branch/handle.rs:1-1 — needs per-shard state and routing helpers.
crates/haematite/src/branch/fork.rs:1-1 — needs multi-shard fork constructor allocating one buffer per shard.
crates/haematite/src/shard/actor.rs:12-25 — ShardActor owns one WalBuffer per shard actor; useful mental model for shard-local write state.
crates/haematite/src/shard/router.rs:1-1 — router is only a placeholder, so branch routing may need a local ShardId abstraction for now.
crates/haematite/src/wal/buffer.rs:116-180 — each shard must get a distinct WalBuffer; do not share the same Arc across shard entries.
context: The shard router is not implemented yet, so there is no canonical ShardId type or key-to-shard function in the codebase.
ShardActor’s structure reinforces that WAL buffers are shard-local mutable state.
Because current_root initially equals fork_point per shard, storing a single global root is insufficient for CN5 unless the API is explicitly single-shard only.
scout.approach: Introduce a small public ShardId type alias in branch/handle.rs or branch/fork.rs (for example usize) unless a project-wide type exists by implementation time. Represent BranchHandle as a map/vector of shard entries and provide routing methods that either take shard_id explicitly or use an injected/simple routing function if tests demand key-based get/put. The multi-shard fork function should iterate the supplied shard roots once, create independent Arc<Mutex<WalBuffer>> values per shard, and store each shard’s own fork_point/current_root.
scout.notes: Do not accidentally clone one WalBuffer/Arc into every shard entry; that would make writes to shard 3 visible in shard 5. Also avoid adding cross-shard locks around the whole fork/handle unless needed for registry bookkeeping; CN5 explicitly rejects cross-shard coordination.

files_changed.note: Added per-shard state, ShardId alias, per-shard root accessors, shard_ids(), and shard-routed get/put/delete.
Added fork_shards() and fork_shards_registered() to build one logical branch from independent shard roots.
Added tests for independent shard roots, distinct shard WAL buffers, and shard-routed reads/writes.

dev.how: BranchHandle coordinates a primary shard plus additional shard entries, each with its own fork-point/current root and distinct Arc<Mutex<WalBuffer>>. Operations take an explicit ShardId and route to that shard’s buffer/root without cross-shard locking or key-router coordination.

checklist.note: Each shard fork has independent roots and WAL buffers, and branch methods route by shard id.

stories.note: Test-environment forks can represent a logical database across independent shard roots.

acceptance.criterion: Each shard in the branch has an independent fork-point root hash

acceptance.criterion: Each shard in the branch has an independent WAL buffer

requirements.title: BranchRegistry for tracking active branch roots
requirements.spec: Define a BranchRegistry type that maintains a HashSet of active branch root hashes. WHEN a BranchHandle is created via fork(), THE SYSTEM SHALL register its root hash in the registry. WHEN a BranchHandle is dropped, THE SYSTEM SHALL deregister its root hash. THE SYSTEM SHALL expose a live_roots() method returning the set of all active branch root hashes for use by the pruner (BRANCH-004).
acceptance: BranchRegistry::new() creates an empty registry
Forking a database registers the branch root in the registry
Dropping a BranchHandle deregisters its root
live_roots() returns all currently active branch root hashes
BranchRegistry is thread-safe (Arc<Mutex<HashSet<Hash>>> or equivalent)

files: crates/haematite/src/branch/registry.rs:1-1 — placeholder for BranchRegistry implementation.
crates/haematite/src/branch/mod.rs:1-15 — add BranchRegistry re-export while preserving declaration-only module style.
crates/haematite/src/branch/handle.rs:1-1 — BranchHandle needs optional registry guard/drop integration.
crates/haematite/src/branch/fork.rs:1-1 — fork constructor should register roots when given a registry.
crates/haematite/src/tree/node.rs:9-10 — Hash implements std::hash::Hash/Eq, so it can be stored in HashSet.
context: Hash already has Eq and std::hash::Hash derives, so std::collections::HashSet<Hash> is directly usable.
The branch registry requested here is in-memory/thread-safe, unlike SnapshotRegistry which is persistent and non-thread-safe.
Drop-based deregistration plus Clone handles requires reference-count semantics; a naive Drop on every clone will deregister too early.
scout.approach: Implement BranchRegistry as a lightweight cloneable wrapper around Arc<Mutex<HashSet<Hash>>> with new(), register(hash), deregister(hash), and live_roots() -> HashSet<Hash>. For fork integration, have the registered BranchHandle own a small guard object containing the root(s), registry clone, and an Arc<()> token; only deregister when the last handle clone is dropped, either by putting the guard in an Arc with a Drop impl on the inner guard or by maintaining per-root reference counts. Re-export BranchRegistry from branch/mod.rs and lib.rs.
scout.notes: The spec says “root hash” singular but R5 says each shard has an independent root. Decide whether live_roots includes all shard fork roots for a branch; for pruner safety, it should, because pruning any live shard root is data loss. If HashSet alone is used, duplicate branches at the same root are tricky: dropping one branch would remove the root while another branch still references it; consider HashMap<Hash, usize> internally while returning HashSet<Hash> from live_roots(), or make the guard reference-counted per root.

files_changed.note: Replaced placeholder with BranchRegistry, root reference counting, live_roots(), drop guard, and registry tests.
Added registry guard storage using Arc so roots deregister only after the last handle clone drops.
Added registry-aware fork constructors that register branch roots.
Re-exported BranchRegistry from the branch module.
Re-exported BranchRegistry from the crate root.

dev.how: BranchRegistry is a cloneable Arc<Mutex<_>> wrapper whose live_roots() returns a HashSet<Hash>. It uses internal HashMap<Hash, usize> counts so duplicate live branches at the same root and cloned handles remain pruning-safe; registry-aware fork helpers attach a drop guard to BranchHandle so roots deregister when the last handle clone drops.
dev.deviation: The spec named HashSet storage, but the scout explicitly noted duplicate-root safety; I used reference counts internally and preserve the required HashSet interface via live_roots(). Registry integration is exposed through fork_registered()/fork_shards_registered() because plain fork(root) has no registry parameter.

acceptance.criterion: BranchRegistry::new() creates an empty registry
acceptance.evidence: crates/haematite/src/branch/registry.rs:16-21 implements `new()` via `Default`; test `new_registry_has_no_live_roots` at registry.rs:100-105 passed.

acceptance.criterion: Forking a database registers the branch root in the registry
acceptance.evidence: The registry-aware fork path `fork_registered(root, &registry)` registers `branch.fork_points()` at crates/haematite/src/branch/fork.rs:22-25; tests `registered_fork_root_is_live_until_handle_drops` at registry.rs:107-119 and `fork_registered_registers_root_until_drop` passed.

acceptance.criterion: Dropping a BranchHandle deregisters its root
acceptance.evidence: `BranchHandle` stores `Option<Arc<BranchRegistryGuard>>` at crates/haematite/src/branch/handle.rs:81-87 and attaches it at handle.rs:259-262; `BranchRegistryGuard::drop` deregisters roots at registry.rs:82-87. Tests at registry.rs:107-119 and :121-133 passed, including clone lifetime behavior.

acceptance.criterion: live_roots() returns all currently active branch root hashes
acceptance.evidence: crates/haematite/src/branch/registry.rs:46-50 returns a `HashSet<Hash>` of live roots; multi-shard test `registered_multi_shard_fork_tracks_every_shard_root` at registry.rs:149-166 verifies all shard roots are reported and then cleared.

acceptance.criterion: BranchRegistry is thread-safe (Arc<Mutex<HashSet<Hash>>> or equivalent)
acceptance.evidence: crates/haematite/src/branch/registry.rs:11-14 defines a cloneable registry backed by `Arc<Mutex<HashMap<Hash, usize>>>`, an equivalent thread-safe live-root set with reference counts; locking is centralized in registry.rs:67-72.

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement merge or conflict resolution — those are BRANCH-002
SHALL NOT implement snapshot registry or time-travel — those are BRANCH-003
SHALL NOT implement snapshot pruning — that is BRANCH-004
SHALL NOT copy tree nodes during fork — fork is O(1) via shared content-addressed store
SHALL NOT implement branch commit (flushing branch WAL to tree) — that is part of merge in BRANCH-002
verification: cargo check -p haematite succeeds with no errors
cargo test -p haematite --lib passes all tests
cargo clippy -p haematite produces no errors
grep -r 'unwrap()\|expect(' crates/haematite/src/branch/ --include='*.rs' -l finds no non-test files
No file in crates/haematite/src/branch/ exceeds 500 lines
